title: Acoustic and social bonding differences between Japanese singing and conversation: A Registered Report replicating Ozaki et al. (2024)
description: Understanding cross-cultural differences between singing and speech is a central goal of the Many Voices project (Savage et al. 2025a). As part of this effort, the present study examined Japanese participants (n=10 for confirmatory acoustic analysis; 15 for exploratory behavioural analysis) singing the traditional folk song Tokyo Ondo or having a conversation about favourite foods. Confirmatory analyses replicated previously reported tendencies (Ozaki et al., 2024): singing was higher in pitch, slower in temporal rate, and used more stable pitches than spoken conversation. Temporal rate showed a large effect (Cohen’s d = 1.6), while pitch height (d = 0.55) and pitch stability (d = 0.39) showed medium effect sizes. Exploratory analyses of relationships between acoustic features and social variables suggested that singing increased participants’ self-reported sense of social bonding, and individuals reporting higher perceived synchrony tended to show greater singing–speech differences. This study provides culturally grounded data of Japanese conversation and traditional singing for cross-cultural vocal research and underscores the importance of including traditional repertoires in studies of acoustic variation. The Many Voices project will continue collecting data from up to 24 additional languages and regions, with a subsequent meta-analysis assessing cross-linguistic consistency in singing–speech differences.

title: Realistic deepfakes are detected better from a single glance than deliberate inspection
description: The rapidly increasing realism of AI-generated faces threatens trust in digital media, as observers often fail to reliably distinguish AI-generated faces from real faces under prolonged viewing. Across three experiments, participants (N = 300) classified faces varying in realism, exposure duration, and feature availability as real or AI-generated. Observers detected AI-generated faces above chance based on 83 ms when external features, such as hair and background, were present, whereas unlimited viewing did not improve detection of realistic AI-generated faces. These findings suggest observers can extract gist-based authenticity-related signals from a single glimpse, but these may be overridden during prolonged evaluative face processing.

title: Minimum Cost Psychological Defense: Theoretical Construction and Anti-Fraud Application
description: Human decision-making under acute stress reveals a critical paradox in applied social psychology: despite possessing adequate declarative risk knowledge, individuals frequently exhibit a knowing-doing gap when confronting high-pressure digital deception. Traditional interventions demanding substantial cognitive resources often fail when deceivers hijack victims' limited cognitive bandwidth via emotional manipulation. To address this, we propose the Minimum Cost Psychological Defense (M.C.P.D.) model. In Study 1, using a mixed-methods design (N = 679), we developed and validated the Public Anti-Fraud Skill Scale. It comprises three dimensions—emotional buffering, proactive defense, and distributed help-seeking—which significantly predicted reduced real-world victimization risk. In Study 2, a double-blind randomized controlled trial (N = 185) evaluated an emergency guide that encodes complex verification tasks into simple implementation intentions. Compared to traditional education, this micro-skill intervention significantly reduced compliance impulses under simulated high-pressure fraud scenarios and shortened decision-making response times, indicating successful cognitive offloading. By transforming declarative knowledge into procedural, automated micro-skills, the M.C.P.D. strategy effectively bypasses executive function limitations. This shifts the anti-fraud paradigm from trait assessment to dynamic behavioral blocking, providing significant contributions to dual-process theory and a scalable tool for practical interventions.

title: Whole-brain anatomical and temporal patterns  hypothesized to be evoked during a false-belief fMRI task
description:
Background: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) investigations of theory of 
mind and false-belief processing have been central to cognitive neuroscience for several 
decades. Drawing on previous fMRI studies, we (1) hypothesize that a task-general brain 
mode (DMB) will emerge during the false-belief task, (2) specify its anatomical properties, 
and (3) hypothesize how it will respond, in terms of blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) 
signal changes, to task conditions in a false-belief task.
Task: The false-belief task involved story processing involving either theory of mind or only 
physical properties, resulting in two conditions: (1) Mental, and (2) Physical.
Anatomical: The spatial patterns for Default Mode B (DMB; Lemon, Headlights, Flying 
Seagull, Tripod and Angel Wings) are provided in Figure 1.
Temporal: DMB is predicted to activate during the Mental condition due to engagement of 
theory of mind processes, and to deactivate during the Physical condition. Both are predicted 
to involve a mid-trial peak activation or deactivation. Details are presented in the text below 
and Figure 3.
Analysis: Constrained Principal Component Analysis for fMRI (fMRI-CPCA), an 
application of multivariate multiple regression with a finite impulse response model of the 
task timing, followed by dimensionality reduction, will be used to test these hypotheses.
Conclusions: For the false-belief task, DMB is hypothesized to emerge and show specific 
spatial and temporal characteristics. Although examined within the context of false-belief 
tasks, this mode is not specific to this task. Rather, it is part of a broader set of task-general 
functional brain modes observed across multiple cognitive tasks. This approach may provide 
insight into cognitive processes detectable by fMRI underlying theory of mind.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of efficacy beliefs in pro-environmental behavior change
description: Efficacy beliefs – individuals’ conviction that their actions can make a meaningful difference – are widely regarded as key psychological drivers of pro-environmental behavior and promising targets for climate interventions. Yet it remains unclear whether changing these beliefs translates into action. We conducted a preregistered systematic review synthesizing longitudinal and interventional evidence (129 studies; N ≈ 65,000) to evaluate whether, and under what conditions, efficacy beliefs promote pro-environmental behavior change. After correcting for publication bias, efficacy interventions showed no meaningful effect on intention and behavior (d = 0.01-0.04), largely because most interventions failed to reliably increase efficacy beliefs. Multi-session interventions showed greater promise, whereas brief, message-based interventions were ineffective in altering pro-environmental action. These findings challenge assumptions about the malleability and behavioral impact of efficacy beliefs and redirect research toward understanding how efficacy beliefs can be durably strengthened and interact with social and structural factors to promote climate action.

title: Development, Content Validation, and Preliminary Accessibility Testing of a Workplace Well-Being Assessment Tool for Workers with Intellectual Disabilities
description:
Background: Workplace well-being is a critical determinant of job retention for people with intellectual disabilities. Despite its importance, no validated instrument exists to assess this construct among these workers. The objective of the present research was therefore to develop a battery of questionnaire tailored to people with intellectual disabilities in sheltered employment to assess workplace well-being and its associated factors. Furthermore, this study aimed to provide evidence regarding the instrument's content validity and initial evidence of its accessibility for the target population.
Method: In a first study, the content of the developed questionnaire battery was evaluated by an expert committee using the Lynn method. The instrument included four indicators of workplace well-being (i.e., burnout, need for recovery, vigor, job satisfaction), job characteristics (i.e., job demands and job resources), and off-duty recovery (i.e., recovery activities and experiences), variables based on the JD-R model, DISC-R model and recovery theories. In a second study, this validated questionnaire was administered to 29 participants to assess its accessibility.
Results: All 80 items developed received content validation by the expert committee. The pilot study conducted with the target population provided initial evidence of the instrument accessibility.
Conclusion: Further research is needed to test the questionnaire with a larger sample to provide psychometric evidence and additional evidence of its accessibility for workers with intellectual disabilities.

title: Subjective and physiological measures reveal emotionally salient moments in virtual everyday life
description:
Emotions in everyday life are dynamic, context-dependent, and socially embedded, yet most psychophysiological research relies on highly controlled and often artificial laboratory paradigms. This raises questions about whether emotional processes observed under such conditions generalize to naturalistic contexts and how subjective and physiological measures relate in everyday settings.

The present study addresses these challenges by combining ethnographically informed virtual reality (VR) stimuli with multimodal measurement of emotional responses. Participants viewed immersive 360° videos depicting everyday social encounters while electrocardiography, skin conductance, facial electromyography, and gaze patterns were recorded. Following each scenario, participants provided affective ratings across multiple dimensions, including valence, arousal, approach–avoidance, and social connectedness.

Across measures, results revealed systematic but context-dependent divergence between subjective and physiological responses. Associations between modalities were generally weak and selective rather than reflecting consistent one-to-one correspondence. Instead, different measures appeared to capture complementary aspects of emotional processing. Furthermore, relationships between physiological signals and affective ratings varied across events, highlighting the role of contextual and social meaning in shaping emotional responses.

These findings suggest that emotional experience in everyday contexts cannot be reduced to single physiological or subjective indicators but is better understood as a multi-component, context-sensitive process. The results underscore the importance of multimodal approaches and ecologically valid designs. More broadly, they demonstrate the potential of immersive VR as a methodological framework for studying naturalistic emotional experiences while maintaining experimental control.

title: Obsessive Thinking About a Loved One in Romantic Love: Eight Perspectives
description: Obsessive thinking about a loved one (i.e., constant, involuntary, repetitive thoughts about the loved one) is a defining feature of romantic love yet remains markedly under-researched. This article provides the first review of obsessive thinking about a loved one in romantic love, organising the disparate literature using an "Eight Perspectives" approach spanning evolutionary history, evolutionary functions, development, mechanisms, ecology, cognitions, emotions, and behaviour. We argue that obsessive thinking about a loved one likely emerged by co-opting mother-infant bonding circuitry, appears across cultures and across the lifespan, and may have evolved to promote proximity-seeking and sustained orientation toward a loved one. Contrary to long-standing claims, the mechanisms underlying obsessive thinking in romantic love are unlikely to be shared with obsessive-compulsive disorder. People who are in love think about their loved one for approximately 50% of their waking hours, and this thinking is associated with romantic (but not companionate) love, distraction, proximity-seeking behaviour, and analgesia. Whether such thinking is experienced as bothersome or enjoyable depends less on its frequency than on companionate love and reciprocation. We conclude by identifying priorities for future research across all eight perspectives.

title: Overall attitudes towards quantum computing are more positive than overall attitudes towards artificial intelligence considering also knowledge levels
description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) has become a major topic of public and scientific debate, whereas public perceptions of quantum computing (QC) remain less understood. In the present study, we aimed to understand if people have different views on AI vs. QC, while also asking them about their perceived knowledge levels in these two domains. Further, we investigated the construct of technology self-efficacy to consider how people in general think they can master new technology. 
In N = 475 German speaking participants from the general population (227 females, 248 males) we observed a) that people report on average more positive attitudes towards QC than towards AI, while b) at the same time reporting less knowledge on QC compared to AI. In mediation models, we observed that the link between technology self-efficacy and more positive AI / QC attitudes is mediated by knowledge levels about AI / QC. We also shed light on further mediation models with negative AI / QC attitudes and pessimism aversion in the context of AI / QC as outcomes. 
The present data could be interpreted in a way that the massive global discussions on AI technology might make people less positive towards this general-purpose technology compared to overall QC attitudes, whereas interestingly the reported knowledge levels about QC are lower than those regarding AI. This is noteworthy, as it is discussed that the fusion of QC and AI technology will represent a powerful merger likely leading to a dramatic increase of scientific discovery, an assumption that received moderate support in the present sample.

title: Foundations of English as a Foreign Language Literacy: Cognitive and Linguistic Contributions to English Reading and Spelling in Young German Learners
description:
Purpose: This longitudinal study investigated the cognitive and linguistic predictors of English as a foreign language (EFL) literacy among elementary school children in Germany, focusing on reading comprehension, pseudoword reading, reading fluency, and real-word and pseudoword spelling.

Method: A sample of 107 students (53 boys, 54 girls; M_age = 10.52, SD = 0.64; 82 L1 German, 25 with other L1) was assessed at the beginning of Grade 3 on cognitive abilities (phonological awareness, phonological short-term memory, working memory, nonverbal intelligence) and linguistic skills (English vocabulary and grammar). Linear mixed-effects models, including age, gender, and language background as covariates, were used to predict six EFL literacy outcomes at the end of Grade 4, accounting for the nested data structure.

Results: Nonverbal intelligence and phonological awareness were robust predictors of EFL reading comprehension, with L2 vocabulary adding a further contribution. Beyond comprehension, distinct skills predicted distinct outcomes: phonological short-term memory predicted pseudoword reading, L2 receptive grammar and vocabulary predicted real-word spelling, and nonverbal intelligence predicted pseudoword spelling. These additional effects align with theoretical models and offer encouraging initial evidence that merits replication. Reading fluency was not predicted by the variables examined. 

Conclusion: The findings underscore the necessity of fostering vocabulary, grammatical knowledge, and phonological processing, alongside general cognitive skills, in EFL literacy instruction for young learners. Adopting a multidimensional perspective can inform targeted literacy support strategies and offer a better understanding of the knowledge bases underlying early EFL literacy in non-English-speaking contexts.

title: Revisiting the Blind Implantation Paradigm: Robustness of False Memory Induction Under Controlled Conditions
description: The Blind Implantation paradigm was introduced as a scalable method for inducing false autobiographical memories without human interaction (Otgaar et al., 2022). We conducted a conceptual replication (N = 348) with a sample three times the size of the original, using a multi-target event design and a non-suggestive control group. Results confirm that the procedure reliably induces false beliefs and memories, supporting the reconstructive nature of memory. However, induction rates (12–15% for beliefs; 8.5% for memories) were substantially lower than in the original study. Furthermore, we found no evidence of an imagination effect, likely attributable to low task compliance. Exploratory analyses of written accounts support the validity of the paradigm and the utilized self-report scales. We discuss the implications for the scalability of memory research, emphasizing the need for comprehensive construct validation to assure differentiation of genuine memory distortion from other experimental effects in unmonitored online settings.

title: An Individual-Level Paradigm for Research on Deception Cues: An Urgent Proposal
description: All psychological theories of deception cues concern explanatory mechanisms at the level of the individual. However, past research on deception cues compares the behavior of truth tellers and liars at the group level. Unless two conditions are met, that the statistical models do not vary (i) across individuals or (ii) across occasions in time, group-level findings cannot accurately describe the behavior of individuals. We show that these conditions are unlikely to hold for deception cues. This means the current group-level research cannot provide a meaningful test of the individual-level mechanisms key to understanding deception cues. In order to draw meaningful conclusions about these individual-level mechanisms, the field needs a wholly different research paradigm, based on repeated measures designs. We outline a proposal for such an individual-level research agenda. Without this development, fundamental questions in deception research will remain unanswered and theories of deception will remain incomplete.

title: Smoking Exposure Application (SEA): Development and Validation of An Augmented Reality Smoking Cessation App
description:
Background: Cue-reactivity is a cornerstone of addiction research; however, laboratory-based investigations often lack ecological validity, failing to generalize to real-world experiences. Augmented reality (AR) technology offers a promising solution by bridging the gap between laboratory settings and real-life environments for cue-exposure interventions. 
Objectives: The present study aimed to: (1) develop and validate a set of three-dimensional (3D) smoking-related objects through user evaluation among smokers; and (2) to incorporate these validated images into a smoking cessation application, the Smoking Exposure Application (SEA), designed to elicit cravings within familiar contexts without physical cigarette exposure.
Method: We developed 21 3D objects, including 10 smoking-related cues (e.g., cigarette, ashtray) and 11 control objects (e.g., pencil, gum pack). Forty-five participants (21 smokers and 24 non-smokers) evaluated the objects using the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM) to rate valence, arousal, dominance, and desire to smoke. Additionally, participants assessed SEA’s naturalness and integration, and perceived object presence using a 0–10 scale, alongside qualitative comments.
Results: Within-group analysis showed that smokers reported significantly higher ratings for smoking-related objects compared to control objects across valence, arousal, dominance, and desire to smoke (p < 0.001). Conversely, non-smokers rated control objects higher than smoking-related objects in valence and arousal. Between-group analysis confirmed that smokers reported significantly higher responses to smoking-related objects than non-smokers, particularly regarding desire to smoke (p < 0.001). 
Conclusions: The 3D dataset effectively elicited smoking craving and associated emotional and regulatory responses (valence, arousal, and dominance) in smokers. The SEA provided an immersive and ecologically valid experience, supporting its potential utility as a digital tool for s

title: No Escape from Behavior in Evaluating Compositionality
description: Evaluating whether a natural or artificial system exhibits compositionality is an empirical problem of active interest in both Cognitive Science and Artificial Intelligence. Because any function admits some compositional analysis, we need theoretical commitments that clearly define the target function for the question to be well-formed. Which function is appropriate depends on one's domain and scope of inquiry, and no single function is inherently privileged without further independent commitments; the only plausibly universal constraint is that the domain must admit at least one primitive-reuse analysis. Given a properly operationalized notion of compositionality, I argue that there is no escape from behavior in asking whether a system exhibits compositionality. From a constitutive perspective, the complete functional profile is sufficient for the verdict. From an empirical perspective, targeted generalization behavior over withheld recombinations is the critical evidence supporting inductive hypotheses about the function. Contra recent literature arguing that the compositionality verdict should be grounded in mechanistic evidence, mechanism is neither a necessary nor a sufficient adjudicator because it is constitutively subordinate to or evidentially dependent on behavior. Instead, the role of mechanistic evidence is best understood as a bootstrap to the inductive process and an aid to the characterization of the target function. Furthermore, settling the _is-compositional_ question rigorously opens up ways for genuine questions _about_ the mechanism and the behavior-mechanism interface to proceed productively. Finally, this discussion highlights that the critical bottleneck for the _is-compositional_ verdict on contemporary systems is data transparency rather than interpretability.

title: Handing over control: A longitudinal study of delegation and disclosure in human-AI interaction
description: The increasing integration of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) into everyday tasks raises important questions about how individuals rely on these systems in the long run. While prior research has primarily examined cross-sectional predictors of AI use, little is known about how willingness to delegate tasks and disclose information to AI develops over time. The present study addresses this gap examining a six-wave longitudinal survey (N = 1,008 at baseline) conducted over ten months with a U.S. sample. We investigated changes in willingness to delegate professional and personal writing tasks to AI, as well as willingness to disclose information to AI, and tested the influence of user-related (experience, frequency of use, attitudes, and knowledge) and AI-related evaluative factors (trust, anthropomorphism, social actor perception). Contrary to expectations, willingness to delegate and disclose decreased over time, with the decline attenuating across waves. Willingness to delegate remained consistently higher for professional than for personal tasks. Use frequency, positive attitudes, trust, and anthropomorphism were associated with greater willingness to delegate, whereas experience and social actor perception showed no consistent effects. Willingness to disclose was similarly shaped by use and attitudinal factors. These findings suggest that human-AI interaction reflects a process of calibration rather than increasing reliance, highlighting the importance of temporal dynamics in understanding AI adoption and use.

title: Individual Differences in Reliance on Generative AI in Schoolwork: The Role of Executive Functioning Difficulties, Perceived Usefulness, and Habitual Use
description: Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) tools are becoming an increasingly common part of adolescents’ everyday and academic life, raising important questions about which individuals may be more likely to rely on them when facing challenges. Drawing on theories of technology adoption, habit formation, and self-regulation, the present study examined a sequential mediation model linking executive functioning (EF) difficulties to reliance on GenAI, defined as a tendency to depend on GenAI rather than one’s own effort or teacher support when encountering academic challenges, through perceived usefulness and habitual use. The model was tested using structural equation modeling in community samples of lower secondary school students (N = 849, Mage = 14.8 years, 49.4% girls) and upper secondary school students (N = 898, Mage = 17.4 years, 55.2% girls). Across both groups, EF difficulties were positively associated with perceived usefulness, habitual use, and reliance on GenAI (rs = .14–.32, ps < .001). Supporting the hypothesized model, the sequential indirect pathway through perceived usefulness and habitual use was significant in both groups (βs = .08–.10, ps < .001). Perceived usefulness emerged as the most consistent mediator, whereas habitual use showed a more limited mediating role despite being strongly associated with reliance (βs = .60–.69, ps < .001). The findings identify EF as an important individual-difference correlate of reliance on GenAI and suggest that perceived usefulness may be a key mechanism linking EF difficulties to reliance. More broadly, the results highlight the role of self-regulatory capacities in adolescents’ reliance on GenAI.

title: Sensory Processing Sensitivity is associated with Heightened Emotional Response, not Rumination when viewing Climate‑Change Media Content
description: Climate change is becoming increasingly recognised as a serious determinant of mental health, with indirect exposure via climate related media eliciting emotional distress and rumination. The present study sought to explore whether sensory processing sensitivity (SPS) moderates responses to climate-related videos, due to higher empathy and depth of processing characterising highly sensitive individuals. In a pre-post experimental design, 119 participants were randomly assigned to view either a climate disaster video or a control weather forecast. Positive affect, negative affect, and rumination were assessed before and after exposure. Results indicated that exposure to climate-related content significantly increased negative affect. SPS moderated this effect, such that individuals higher in sensitivity showed greater increases in negative affect in the climate condition. Nature connectedness also emerged as a significant positive predictor of negative affect, whereas climate change anxiety did not significantly predict outcomes in the final models. Positive affect and rumination were primarily explained by baseline levels and were not significantly influenced by condition or SPS. These findings suggest that negatively valenced climate-related media may elicit distress-related emotional responses, particularly among individuals high in sensory sensitivity, indicating that this dimension is of relevance when studying distressing nature-related situations.

title: Interictal Epileptiform Discharges and Long-Term Neurodevelopmental Outcomes in Children with Early-Onset Epilepsy: A Systematic Review and Narrative Synthesis
description:
Background: Children with early-onset epilepsy (seizure onset ≤ 5 years) are at elevated risk of adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes, including cognitive impairment, intellectual disability, and autism spectrum features. Seizure burden alone provides limited prognostic information. Interictal epileptiform discharges (IEDs), spike or sharp-wave complexes occurring between clinical seizures on electroencephalography (EEG), are common in paediatric epilepsy and may reflect brain network instability relevant to neurodevelopment. Whether interictal EEG features recorded in early childhood are associated with long-term neurodevelopmental outcomes remains unclear.
Methods: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with a prospectively registered protocol (PROSPERO: CRD420251162420) and reported per PRISMA 2020 guidelines. MEDLINE, Embase, PubMed, PsycINFO, and Scopus were searched from 1966 to October 2025. Longitudinal studies examining interictal EEG features in children with epilepsy onset ≤ 5 years, with neurodevelopmental outcomes assessed ≥12 months after baseline EEG, were eligible. Risk of bias was assessed using QUIPS; certainty of evidence using GRADE.
Results: Of 1,613 records identified, four longitudinal studies met inclusion criteria (epilepsy contexts: infant-onset focal epilepsy, Sturge–Weber syndrome, epileptic encephalopathies, cryptogenic generalised epilepsies). Across all four studies, greater severity or disruption of interictal EEG activity showed a directional association with poorer neurodevelopmental outcomes. All four studies were rated at moderate to high risk of bias. Certainty of evidence was low to very low across all outcome domains.
Conclusions: Directional evidence links interictal EEG severity to neurodevelopmental outcomes in early-onset epilepsy. However, the evidence base is too sparse, insufficiently standardised, and of too low methodological quality to support definitive prognostic inference. Standardised, state-resolved IED m

title: The Joint Effects of Measurement Error and Model Misspecification on Statistical Interactions
description: Measurement error in predictors and unmodelled interaction effects pose recurring challenges in psychological research, yet their joint consequences for regression analysis have not been analytically characterized. We show that classical measurement error and the omission of a true interaction effect are not independent sources of bias, but become entangled such that their combined effects cannot be recovered by applying standard corrections for either problem alone. Specifically, we derive closed-form expressions for the probability limits of OLS regression weights under joint measurement error and interaction omission, characterize the implied asymptotic residual structure, and establish expressions for model fit and prediction error for infinite samples. A key finding is that the residual contributions from measurement error and interaction omission can partially cancel each other out, so that prediction error reduces marginally while leaving coefficient bias large. As a consequence, qualitatively different models can become difficult to distinguish by standard fit metrics, even in large samples. Furthermore, we demonstrate via Monte Carlo simulation that models with more biased estimates can exhibit lower estimator variance, meaning misspecified models may dominate in both inferential and model selection procedures. These results have direct implications for the replicability and interpretability of linear regression models in psychological research.

title: Investigating prospective associations between household food insecurity and eating disorder pathology in young people in the UK: Evidence from Understanding Society
description: Adolescence is a critical developmental period for the emergence of eating disorders, and the rising prevalence of food insecurity in the UK, particularly among families with children, raises important questions about whether this may contribute to the development of eating disorder pathology. Despite evidence linking food insecurity to eating disorder risk, adolescents remain underrepresented in this literature. This study investigates prospective associations of household food insecurity with dieting/weight loss behaviours and negative appearance evaluations in a UK cohort of adolescents. The study uses data from the UK Household Longitudinal Study (Waves 13 (2021-2023) and 15 (2023-2025)). Household food insecurity was assessed at Wave 13, while dieting/weight-loss behaviours and appearance evaluations were assessed among adolescents in Wave 15. Associations were investigated using univariable and multivariable modified Poisson (for dieting/weight loss behaviours) and linear (for appearance concerns) regression models controlling for socio-demographics, main carer mental health, ethnicity, financial strain, and urban/rural residence. The analytical sample included 1140 participants (52% female, 18.8% ethnic minority). In the fully adjusted model, there was a weak association between household food insecurity and later dieting/weight loss behaviours in adolescents (risk ratio = 1.07, 95% CI: 0.99 – 1.16). There was no evidence that greater food insecurity was associated with increased appearance concerns in adolescents in any models.  While reducing food insecurity should remain a public health priority, the present findings provide weak evidence that food insecurity represents a risk factor for dieting/weight loss behaviours among UK adolescents, and no evidence that it is associated with later appearance concerns.

title: Developing a measure for the need for sex
description: The chapter “Developing a measure of the need for sex” presents results from a research program that aimed at developing a picture-story measure of the need for sex (n Sex) through an experimental-manipulation, measure, indicator, and criterion (EMMIC) validation approach. The resulting coding system, which also includes a measure of sexual conflict, is comprised of categories that were reliably sensitive to motive arousal across 6 studies with more than 800 participants. Findings demonstrating the measure’s validity in terms of associations with affective processes accompanying experimental motive arousal and in terms of predicting a behavioral outcome are presented. Results documenting n Sex’s convergent and discriminant validity with other implicit and explicit motive measures and with participant gender are also reported.

title: The Repeatable Social Stress Test (ReSST): a guide and validation of a novel paradigm to elicit repeatable stress responses in humans
description: Stress induction tasks have been widely used to understand human stress mechanisms, yet are limited for assessing repeated or extended stress events, especially within-subject changes. Here, we provide a guide for implementation of the Repeatable Social Stress Test (ReSST), accompanied by validation results in healthy volunteers. The ReSST is developed to induce repeated stress responses both within and across sessions in a hybrid format, consisting of adaptions of the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) and the Iowa Singing Social Stress Test (I-SSST) and two novel, closely matched no-stress control conditions. Video recordings designed to remind participants of the social-evaluative component of the stress induction are used for repeating stress within sessions. The ReSST was validated in 66 healthy volunteers aged 19-58 who completed 4 sessions (2 stress, 2 control). Compared to controls, both stress induction conditions elicited comparable, robust increases in subjective stress, heart rate, and cortisol, as well as marked changes in affect. Sex differences emerged, with women exhibiting stronger subjective responses. Cortisol responses did not significantly differ by sex. Stress reminders successfully evoked subjective and physiological responses within sessions, demonstrating reliable stress reactivation beyond the initial induction. In sum, the ReSST provides researchers with a validated, repeatable tool for inducing robust stress responses within and across sessions, enabling study of recurring stress in experimental settings. To accompany our guide, we provide a detailed step-by-step protocol and materials’ overview shared on OSF. Finally, we reflect on our experiences designing and implementing the ReSST in the context of broader literature.

title: Developmental changes in attention to faces in naturalistic family scenes
description: Many studies have investigated children’s attentional preferences to faces of different ages and genders. However, this research has usually investigated these preferences using pictures of faces in isolation without any visual context and with a focus on particular age groups. This overlooks the fact that we usually encounter people’s faces in messy, natural environments in the context of other people. In the current study, we investigated whom children and adults preferentially attend to when presented with naturalistic family pictures across early childhood. One- to twelve-year-old children (n = 259) and adults (n = 22) were presented with 36 pictures of different family constellations while their eye gaze was tracked. The results indicate that children below the age of three shifted from an early preference for adult faces to a preference for child faces, and they attended more to male than female adults. Adults between 20 and 30 years of age shifted from no preference to a preference for faces of young children. Older children did not show a preference for any particular demographic. Thus, across childhood, social interests change, potentially explained by a person’s physical needs and interests.

title: Some Religious and Spiritual Experiences Associated with Depression Resemble Hypomania/Mania
description: This paper explores whether some religious and spiritual experiences (RSEs) associated with depression resemble hypomania/mania. Content analysis and phenomenological analysis were used to characterize first-person RSE reports associated with depression (N = 249). Findings reveal that some RSEs that follow depression are characterized by long-duration states (lasting days to weeks) of positive emotion, cognitive clarity, and a sense that the external environment is imbued with extra positive emotional and perceptual qualities (e.g., love, beauty, light, vividness). These long-duration positive states are not always preceded by discrete visionary or hallucinatory phenomena, suggesting that hypomanic/manic-like experiences, in addition to psychotic-like experiences, bear relevance to the study of RSEs. Some RSEs that resemble hypomania reportedly annihilate depression or prevent its recurrence. Other reports of recurrent RSEs associated with depression refer to mental health and social problems. Relational features of certain RSEs, such as experiences of unconditional love, acceptance, and support, may relate to better outcomes associated with RSEs relative to typical psychopathological experiences. This interpretation is consistent with the idea that RS traditions function in part, like psychotherapeutic traditions, to promote mental well-being and to protect against psychosocial dysfunction.

title: Neural Language Models’ sensitivity to long-distance dependencies and their island constraints is not universal: a comparison between English and Dutch humans and models
description: A much-debated question in linguistics is whether learning language, specifically grammar, requires a language-specific learning capacity or can be achieved from input alone through domain-general mechanisms. The current study adds to this debate by testing whether Neural language models (NLMs), which learn solely from input and their non-linguistic inductive biases, without any built-in linguistic representations, can model the wh-island constraint. We improve on previous research by testing both NLMs and human participants in the same experimental set-up and directly comparing their results, and doing this cross-linguistically in both English and Dutch. Our results show that the NLMs can successfully model long-distance filler-gap dependencies and the wh-island constraint thereon in English but not in Dutch, opposing the arguments in favor of learning from input alone through domain-general mechanisms.

title: Who wrote the story? AI authorship beliefs alter the brain's semantic prediction
description: People evaluate information differently depending on whether it is believed to originate from another human or from artificial intelligence. Whether such beliefs also alter the neural mechanisms underlying language comprehension remains unknown. We tested whether attributed AI authorship changes predictive semantic processing during reading. Fifty-seven adults read two stories while their EEG was recorded. Before each story, participants were informed that it had been written either by a human or generated by AI, although the stories were identical across conditions. One quarter of sentences ended with semantically incongruent words, established to elicit a strong cortical N400 component. Semantic incongruities yielded a weaker centro-parietal N400 when the stories were attributed to AI than when attributed to a human, indicating weaker semantic prediction despite identical linguistic input. This effect was moderated by prior experience with AI-content accuracy: readers who reported mostly experience with inaccurate AI content exhibited weaker semantic predictions for AI-attributed than for human-attributed stories, compared to readers who had prior experience with accurate AI content and more comparable N400 across conditions. Thus, beliefs about communicative source shape language comprehension: presenting a story as generated by AI changes the neural mechanisms of predictive processing, and these appear to adapt with experience.

title: Associations between social cognition measures and alcohol use across age and sex: a network approach
description: Background: Despite the importance of social factors in the initiation and escalation of alcohol use, social processes related to alcohol use severity are typically studied in isolation. Because real-world social processes occur concurrently and may interact with one another as well as with age and sex, a network approach may provide a more ecologically valid framework. Hence, we used a network approach to examine associations between different social cognition measures and alcohol use severity, while also assessing the roles of age and sex. Methods: Alcohol users (N=936; 16-81yrs) completed the social and conformity subscales of the Drinking Motives Questionnaire Revised, the Social Reward Questionnaire, the Social Attunement Questionnaire, and measures of alcohol use severity. Using network analysis, we examined unique associations between social measures and alcohol use severity, the moderating role of age, and sex differences. Results: Alcohol use severity was linked to social drinking motives and sociability (i.e., the tendency to participate in social gatherings). In the age-moderated network, associations were largely stable across age, except for the association between sociability and alcohol use severity, which was weaker in older participants. We found no sex differences in the pattern of associations. Discussion: Alcohol use severity was primarily associated with general social engagement, while more specific social-cognitive measures were only indirectly related. Social drinking contexts may therefore be a key pathway to greater severity, especially in younger drinkers. Associations did not differ by sex, suggesting previously reported sex differences may reflect differences in prevalence, rather than sex moderating social factors.

title: Temporal interval judgments are affected by stimulus order but not spatial distance
description:
Abstract
Judgments of time are often sensitive to the spatial context of the relevant stimuli as well as their action contingencies. This results in well-known effects of spatial separation on time judgments, such as in the Kappa effect. However, whether and how the egocentric distance of a stimulus modulates how we judge or reproduce intervals remains debated. Across seven experiments, combining experiments in the physical lab and in virtual reality, we tested whether the egocentric distance of visual stimuli influences their reproduction, categorization or discrimination. These tests involved supra-second intervals (range of 1 to 3 seconds) and stimuli at distances of 60 to 240 cm from the body. In none of the experiments were the reproduced duration, perceptual sensitivity or response bias significantly affected by egocentric distance. The respective effect sizes were small and Bayes factors consistently favored the absence of a distance effect. However, in two-stimulus discrimination tasks, we observed robust effects of stimulus order, whereby perceptual sensitivity and response bias varied with the order of test- and reference stimuli. These results show that under static viewing conditions judgements of time intervals remain invariant to spatial depth and thereby place boundary conditions on claims of any obligatory space-time coupling in time perception. By bridging across the physical and virtual lab, we provide a useful foundation for future timing research in virtual and augmented reality.

Keywords: time judgment, time perception, interval reproduction, interval discrimination, temporal order effects, peripersonal space, embodied cognition, virtual reality.

title: Sex Bias in a Gold-Standard Autism Assessment: An Analysis of 19,569 Children and Adults
description:
Importance. Autistic females are consistently underidentified relative to males. Whether item-level sex bias in the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS), the gold standard observational assessment of autism spectrum disorder, contributes to this disparity across the full developmental and language range remains unknown. 

Objectives. To examine differential item functioning (DIF) by sex in ADOS algorithm items across all modules. 

Design. Cross-sectional study. 

Setting. Item-level ADOS data were drawn from the NIMH Data Archive, Simons Simplex Collection, Simons Foundation Powering Autism Research for Knowledge, and Autism Inpatient Collection. 

Participants. Individuals with item-level ADOS or ADOS-2 data who were administered the age-appropriate module. 

Exposure. Sex (female vs. male) 

Main Outcome and Measures. Sex-based DIF was examined separately within each of 8 module and language-level combinations, covering 112 algorithm items total. For each combination, a graded response item response theory model was fit to the item set, and DIF was evaluated using likelihood ratio test approach. 

Results. A total of 19,569 individuals (14,807 male [75.7%]; 4,760 female [24.3%]) aged 12 months to 75 years were included. Of 112 items analyzed, eleven unique items showed significant sex DIF after correcting for type I error. No significant DIF was detected in the Toddler Module, Module 1 Low Language, or Module 2 after correction. Significant DIF was detected in Module 1 High Language (3 items) and Module 3 (1 item), with Module 4 showing the greatest number of significant items (7 of 14). Overall, adult females tended to be underscored on social communication items and overscored on items concerning restricted and repetitive behaviors and interests. All effect sizes were small except one (stereotyped language, Module 1 High Language). 

Conclusions and Relevance. These findings suggest that the ADOS is largely robust to sex-based measurement bias for youn

title: The Dual Lens Model of Behavior: A Conceptual Framework for Integrating Functional Behavior Assessment and Nervous System State
description:
Traditional Functional Behavior Assessments (FBAs) excel at isolating the environmental
contingencies that maintain challenging behaviors over time. However, by focusing primarily on
external consequences, the standard operant paradigm often overlooks the biological, interoceptive,
and neurodevelopmental mechanics that lower behavioral thresholds and drive initial behavioral
activation. This paper introduces the Dual Lens Model of Behavior (DLM), a theoretical framework
designed to conceptually unify operant learning histories with internal nervous system states. The
DLM posits that behavioral topographies emerge from the reciprocal interaction between external
reinforcement variables (Lens 1) and internal organismic state-regulation profiles (Lens 2). By
integrating theoretical insights from radical behaviorism, interoceptive neuroscience, allostatic
predictive processing, and trauma-informed care, the model establishes a structured methodology to
distinguish among reinforcement-driven, state-driven, and interaction-driven behavioral
phenotypes. Furthermore, this synthesis offers specific, falsifiable predictions for intervention
outcomes, provides a systematic four-step clinical assessment workflow, and presents a hypothetical
conceptual illustration demonstrating the framework's practical utility. The DLM outlines a
common scientific taxonomy to bridge the historic divide between behavioral analysts and
neurobiological clinicians, with the objective of minimizing iatrogenic harm in therapeutic and
educational environments.
Keywords: functional behavior assessment, interoception, allostasis, behavior analysis, autism,
trauma-informed care, nervous system regulation, clinical workflow

title: A Developmental Map of Pathogen-Avoidance Behaviors
description:
Despite the significant costs of early-life infections, young children show little behavioral avoidance of pathogens. This phenomenon raises as-of-yet unanswered questions about when anti-pathogen psychology develops. The present cross-sectional study examined parent-reported observations of 39 behaviors from infancy through middle childhood, based on reports from 3,050 parents from the UK and US. Across fine-grained monthly age intervals (0–120 months), distinct developmental trajectories were identified and then grouped into four clusters based on temporal patterns. Behaviors that increased pathogen exposure—such as mouthing and contact with dirty objects—were most frequent during infancy and toddlerhood. Hygiene routines and disgust responses emerged later and remained stable across the observed age range. Two age-related transitions, around the second and fourth years, marked shifts from early exploratory and risk-prone behaviors toward increasingly organized pathogen-avoidant ones. Together, these findings provide a developmental overview of anti-pathogen psychology and clarify when children begin to act in ways that reduce pathogen exposure.

Keywords: pathogen avoidance; development; disgust; hygiene; behavioral immune system

title: Free Will to Imagine: Creative Agency as a Metacognitive Feeling
description: People routinely feel creative ownership over mental processes that are demonstrably shaped by forces outside themselves. We introduce creative agency—the sense of authorship and free will one experiences during creative cognition—as an undertheorized metacognitive feeling and develop a measure of it in the domain of music-evoked imagination. In an initial study (N = 247) and pre-registered replication (N = 219) with Prolific participants, we identify a reliable structure consisting of six distinct but related factors of creative agency and free will in imagination with good psychometric properties. Across studies, creative agency beliefs are robustly associated with absorption in music, musical reward sensitivity, and musical sophistication, suggesting that creative agency is integral to aesthetic experience rather than merely to creative production. These findings establish creative agency as a multidimensional construct characterized by stable individual differences, with implications for understanding aesthetic engagement, metacognition, creativity, and the subjective experience of authorship.

title: Cognitive Functions versus Psychological States in Juggling Performance: A Cross-Sectional Study from the TuJuggling Trial
description:
Background: Juggling is a complex sensorimotor skill, yet the cognitive and psychological correlates of performance remain poorly understood. This study examined the relative contribution of cognitive functions (working memory, visuospatial memory, attentional/inhibitory control) versus psychological states (flow, positive and negative affect) to juggling performance, and tested whether surface stability and flow moderated, and flow mediated, the cognition-performance relationship.
Methods: A total of 191 physical education students (35% female; age 20.6 ± 2.8 years) completed cognitive assessments, flow and affect questionnaires, and a juggling battery stratified by technique (air-toss vs. ground-bounce) and surface (stable vs. unstable). Regression, moderation, and bootstrapped mediation models tested direct, moderated, and indirect effects, adjusting for covariates. Sensitivity analyses restricted the sample to juggling-naïve participants.
Results: No cognitive functions significantly was associated with performance. Flow state, by contrast, consistently associated with performance across total, air-toss, and surface-stratified scores (β = 0.16-0.23), with the strongest fit among all variables examined. Neither affect dimension showed direct effects, and surface stability did not moderate the cognition-performance association. Mediation analyses showed cognitive functions was not associated with flow, whereas both positive (β = 0.46) and negative affect (β = −0.21) significantly associated with flow, which mediated their indirect effects on total and air-toss performance.
Conclusions: Flow state was the central psychological determinant of juggling performance, mediating the affect-outcome link while cognition showed no comparable role. In-task attentional absorption may be more proximally tied to motor skill execution than cognitive resources, with implications for designing juggling-based interventions in physical education.

title: MRIHeadScan: An Automated Pipeline for Quantifying Structural Brain Asymmetry and Statistical Rarity
description: Structural brain asymmetry is a key indicator of neurodevelopmental and pathological states, yet its quantification in large-scale datasets is often confounded by registration errors, site-induced variance, and statistical circularity. In this paper, MRIHeadScan is presented, an automated, containerized neuro-morphometry suite designed to address these challenges. MRIHeadScan stabilizes non-linear spatial normalization (FNIRT) for high-asymmetry brains using robust initialization strategies, achieving a 96% registration success rate on an expanded multi-site composite cohort (N = 2,146) from the ABIDE I and ABIDE II datasets across 37 acquisition sites. To mitigate scanner-induced site-bias without introducing diagnostic bias, the pipeline implements a control-targeted statistical harmonization strategy (reducing the F-statistic for site variance from 15.43 to 0.50). Using a dynamically generated symmetric MNI152 template and Jacobian modulation, a strong structural convergence is demonstrated between native-space geometric proxies and normalized Voxel-Based Morphometry (VBM) gray matter asymmetry indices (r = 0.603, p < 0.001), confirming that the normalized pipeline accurately preserves native anatomical geometry. While cohort-level comparisons revealed no significant group difference in Global Gray Matter Asymmetry between ASD and control subjects (p = 0.5249, Mann-Whitney U), a strict Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) normative modeling approach was utilized to examine individualized structural anomalies. By mapping FDR-corrected voxel-wise outliers (|Z| > 3.1) into independent 3D spatial clusters, it is shown that the ASD cohort exhibits a systematically elevated burden of individualized structural deviations compared to typical controls, measured by Outlier Cluster Mass (p = 1.90 × 10^-5, Mann-Whitney U). These findings provide a rigorous, highly scalable computational framework for detecting individualized, neuroanatomically verified structural ano

title: Arts, cultural, and creative engagement in the United States: a cluster analysis of daily time use patterns
description: Given that arts engagement may have benefits for individuals and communities, it is important to understand everyday arts behaviours in the population. While existing research has looked at predictors of engagement, we lack a more nuanced understanding of engagement as part of people’s daily routines. We used the American Time Use Survey (ATUS); a continuous cross-sectional survey measuring peoples’ activities during the last 24 hours. Combining data from 2021-2024, we explored three domains of arts engagement (participatory, receptive, reading) on weekdays vs weekends. We determined when in the day people engaged in these domains, quantified how daily patterns clustered in the population, and described the individual characteristics associated with clusters using sequence analysis, hierarchical clustering, and multinomial logistic regression. Among those who participated in arts activities (n=9,442) on weekdays or weekends, most were “casual engagers”, alongside large groups of “nighttime readers” and “afternoon creators”. On weekdays, there was also a small group of “daytime readers”. On weekends, in contrast, there were larger groups of “morning mixed engagers” and “all-day audiences”. These behavioural patterns differed across sociodemographic groups, with level of education and employment status most consistently linked to group membership. Researchers, policymakers, and practitioners could use our findings to understand potential audiences and help identify strategies to increase arts engagement.

title: Predictive models of mental health based on resting-state functional connectivity show limited generalizability across late childhood and early adolescence
description:
Late childhood and adolescence are marked by substantial reorganization of the brain’s
functional connectome and the emergence of many mental health problems. Although resting-
state functional connectivity (rsFC) has been widely used to predict mental health and related
behavioral phenotypes, it remains unclear whether such models generalize across development
or instead reflect age-specific brain-behavior mappings. Here, we tested the developmental
generalizability of whole-brain rsFC-based predictive models of mental health and cognitive
ability across three timepoints spanning ages 9-14 years in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive
Development Study. Using out-of-sample prediction and cross-timepoint model transfer, we
found that rsFC significantly predicted concurrent mental health and cognitive ability within
each age group, but models trained at one age often generalized poorly to data acquired 2-4
years earlier or later, particularly for mental health. In addition to this limited transfer, we
demonstrate developmental shifts in the associations between individual functional
connections and mental health and cognitive ability. These changes occurred alongside broader
reorganization of the functional connectome, including age-related differences in global
covariance structure and cortical network segregation. Together, our findings indicate that
brain-behavior mappings of mental health are highly dynamic across late childhood and
adolescence and may challenge the assumption that predictive models or candidate neural
biomarkers for mental health disorders derived at a single age remain valid across development.

title: Fixation durations are more than a correlate of an artificial neural network parameter
description: In a recent study, Sulewski et al. 1 concluded that “[E]ye-movement timing decisions are shaped by memory-encoding demands rather than by perceptual processing limits.” Their evidence for such a conclusion, however, relies primarily on coarsely described correlations with some aspects of artificial neural networks (ANN’s). Their measurements of magnetoencephalography (MEG) do not help their case, especially because these cortex-centric measurements do not differentiate between foveal and extrafoveal processing, and also because the authors do not acknowledge the well-known properties of post-saccadic visual reafferent responses in the brain. In reality, the neurobiology of active vision suggests that fixation durations may ultimately be much more dictated by (extrafoveal) visual reafferent responses, as well as subsequent salience computations, in structures like the superior colliculus (SC) than even the earliest visual cortical areas. An overreliance on (cortex-centric) ANN’s without biological insights in this case does not help either the “neuro” or the “AI” aspects of neuroAI.

title: The Role of Human Agency in Human-AI Co-Creativity
description: Large language models (LLMs) are popular tools for creative ideation, but have been shown to homogenize outputs across users. We test if approaching an AI tool with high human (vs. low) human agency can mitigate this homogenization effect by encouraging people to use AI to augment their creativity, rather than offload it. Participants were experimentally assigned to one of three conditions (high-agency approach with AI access, low-agency approach with AI access, or a human-only control) and generated creative uses for everyday objects. Contrary to our expectations, ideas did not differ in individual-level quality (overall creativity, originality, and usefulness). However, a preregistered similarity-to-centroid analysis and an exploratory cluster analysis provided convergent evidence of AI-induced homogenization among the low-agency condition. Thus, while AI has enabled greater ideational fluency, our research suggests the degree of agency with which people approach their AI tool has downstream consequences on collective creativity.

title: Fine-tuning LLMs for Detecting Negative Self-Referential Language in Smartphone Text among Youth with Depression
description: Adolescent smartphone language provides a lens into negative self-referential thinking, which is central to major depressive disorders (MDD). Prior studies have linked language features, including negative sentiment and first-person pronouns, to mood and depression, suggesting that naturalistic language may identify who is at risk and when that risk is greatest. However, studies of adolescent smartphone social communication have typically relied on rule-based models not validated for heterogeneous, context-sensitive language. To address this gap, we determined whether transformer models including LLMs optimized detection of depression risk in extensive adolescent smartphone text data. In this study, 223 adolescents (AgeMean=16.43 years, current MDD=37, remitted MDD=103, healthy controls=83) installed a smartphone app, which prompted participants to provide mood ratings once a day and acquired all keyboard inputs over 12 months (Mean text entries per participant=17,683). Ten thousand text entries were double-coded for entry-level sentiment (positive, neutral, negative) and self-reference for training and testing traditional rule-based approaches (VADER, pronoun counts) and transformer approaches, including GPT-4-mini. In the full dataset, between-participant associations with depressive symptoms and within-participant relations to daily mood and depressive episodes were examined. Fine-tuned transformer models best aligned with human-coded sentiment labels (F1GPT4-MINI =0.84, F1VADER=0.59) and accurately detected self-reference (F1T5-BASE =0.97). Adolescents with current and remitted MDD exhibited more transformer-based negative self-referential language than healthy controls (ORs=1.38, 1.26). Increased negative self-referential language predicted worse next-day mood (β=-0.033, p<0.001) and higher likelihood of next-week depressive episodes (OR=1.67, 95% CI = [1.06, 2.61], p=0.028), although the association with depressive episodes was not robust to sensitivity ana

title: Emotional and physical abuse have dissociable impacts on punishment learning and task engagement during adolescence
description: Childhood adversity is a robust risk factor for psychopathology, and growing evidence implicates developmental alterations in reward and punishment learning as potent mechanisms shaping this vulnerability. However, little is known about how specific types of adversity impact different learning processes, particularly during the sensitive adolescent period. To examine effects of different types of adversity on the development of valenced learning processes, we combined a subtype-specific approach to childhood adversity, a behavioral task and reinforcement learning (RL) model that capture distinct component processes of learning, and a longitudinal design in youth aged 9-17 (n = 166). Task performance metrics included traditional summary scores and RL-derived estimates for reward learning rate, punishment learning rate, win/loss frequency sensitivity, and go bias (general tendency to play). Analyses probing adversity subtypes and component learning processes revealed dissociable effects:  emotional abuse attenuated normative developmental increases in punishment learning rate, and physical abuse attenuated normative increases in both go bias and a summary score of reward learning (FDR < .05). In contrast, analyses relying on a gross summary score of net success and composite variables representing broad adversity dimensions (abuse vs. neglect) obscured these effects, highlighting the need for fine-grained characterization of both adversity and learning processes. Additionally, adversity did not predict differences in net success, suggesting that youth high and low in adversity reach similar behavioral outcomes through divergent learning strategies. This study is among the first to identify unique effects of different adversity types on specific component processes of valenced learning during adolescence.

title: Model-based evidence for task-dependent representations in psychological similarity and working memory
description: A central question in visual working memory (VWM) is what psychological representation underlies performance. Standard measurement models assume recall error spreads over the physical stimulus space (e.g., a von Mises distribution on a color wheel), whereas the Target Confusability Competition model (Schurgin et al., 2020) proposes that error should instead be scaled by psychophysical similarity. Recent replications, however, find little benefit from similarity-based adjustments to VWM models (Oberauer, 2023; Tomić & Bays, 2024). One limitation of this debate is that similarity is often treated as a separate input—measured in an independent task or approximated parametrically—rather than inferred jointly with the cognitive processes that generate behavior. We address this limitation by developing Bayesian generative models that infer latent stimulus representations of oriented lines based on responses in perceptual reproduction, memory reproduction, and similarity comparison tasks (Tomić & Bays, 2024). The inferred representations deviate systematically from physical orientation, but in task-dependent ways: reproduction tasks show a bias towards the vertical meridian, whereas similarity judgments show attraction toward the cardinal orientations. Using Bayesian model comparison, we find that the task-specific representations provide a better explanation than a common-cause model that assumes a shared representation. This indicates mental representations should be modeled as part of task-specific generative processes, not assumed from similarity judgments alone. We conclude by highlighting two future research directions: attempting to unify our task-specific representations with additional cognitive processes, or attempting to catalog families of task-dependent representations by exploring different cognitive process models.

title: Empathy shapes multimodal child-directed communication and children’s language comprehension
description: Effective communication requires speakers to tailor what they say, and how they say it, to their audience. Empathy may support this process, but it remains unclear whether empathic speakers adapt multimodal signals (speech, prosody, and gesture) in ways that benefit recipient understanding. We examined this question in child-directed communication. In Experiment 1, 46 broadcast-trained Chinese speakers produced semi-spontaneous programmes for child and adult audiences. Higher-empathy speakers showed stronger child-directed adaptation across lexical–syntactic, prosodic and gestural channels, including more audience-oriented lexical choices, enhanced pitch modulation, and more frequent and salient representational gestures. In Experiment 2, 221 children (7–11 years) viewed a subset of these materials from Experiment 1 for a comprehension task. Children understood child-directed programmes better when they were produced by higher-empathetic speakers, whereas empathy-related differences were minimal for adult-directed programmes. Cross-experimental mediation analyses showed that stimulus-level multimodal cues accounted for part of the association between speakers’ empathy and children’s comprehension. This indirect association was positive in child-directed programmes but negative in adult-directed programmes, indicating that empathy benefits children’s comprehension when speakers’ adaptations are aligned with children’s needs. These findings suggest that empathy operates as a higher-order communicative capacity, shaping audience-specific multimodal behaviour with consequences for children’s understanding.

title: Researchers in the behavioural and brain sciences can use e-values to safely optionally stop during data collection
description: This paper is about a central problem facing researchers in the behavioural and brain sciences: limited resources constrain the amount of data we can collect. To address this familiar concern, we describe a new method for monitoring evidence during data collection to prevent wasted effort and resources. An emerging statistical framework called safe statistics, or anytime-valid inference, provides a statistically valid way to optionally stop (or continue) data collection. Before we describe this new framework, we briefly consider a few existing alternatives aimed at solving the same problems including group sequential designs and sequential Bayesian statistics. We point to other reviews of these approaches and discuss some reasons why uptake among behavioural and brain scientists has been limited. We then make the case that the safe statistics framework is well-suited to some of the common challenges that researchers face. We show how the framework works in a set of case studies and discuss its current limitations. We also provide code researchers can use from the safestats R package along with a companion shiny application to help the interested reader get started.

title: Comments on “Animal personality is a class of variation not a set of traits” by Réale, Dall, and Dingemanse (2026). Praise, and a pathway for integrating the study of personality evolution
description: Réale, Dall, and Dingemanse have contributed a valuable commentary, both in terms of clarifying how to (and not to) consider “personality traits” and in, perhaps unknowingly, providing a glimmer of a bridge that can unify the study of animal and human personality. My comments reveal the bridge in all its glory.

title: Inference of light-source positions and of transparency in brightness illusion displays (Digital Typeset Edition)
description: Although many researchers have attempted to explain brightness illusions, no consensus agreement has yet been reached.  I here report that two factors are essential to the study of brightness illusions, and propose a Helmholtzian explanation for the illusions based on differences of logical interpretations for the displays.  The factors that there are: (1) a possibility that our visual system unconsciously infers three light-source positions for segments in the displays (the front of the segments, the segments themselves, and the area behind the segments) and (2) a possibility that our visual system interprets a non-transparent achromatic segment not only as a non-transparent surface illuminated from the side, but also as a transparent segment illuminated from behind, a shade, a cast shadow, a self-luminous segment, or a space.  If our visual system take the factors into account and selects the more likely interpretations out of possible interpretations, it is unsurprising that segments that are physically identical gray is perceived as a different brightness in each situation.

title: The Relationship Between Trauma Type, Coping Style, and Posttraumatic Symptomatology: A Retrospective Online Study
description:
Background: Previous research has shown that interpersonal trauma is associated with higher rates of posttraumatic stress symptoms, with coping responses playing a vital role in individual differences in symptom presentation. The present online study examined differential associations between trauma type, coping styles, and trauma-related symptomatology to deepen understanding of variation in trauma-related symptomatology.
Methods: The sample included N = 300 participants who reported a traumatic life event within the past 18 months, assessed with the Life Events Checklist for DSM-5 (LEC-5). Events were classified into interpersonal and non-interpersonal trauma. Participants completed retrospective questions assessing posttraumatic coping strategies, current levels of posttraumatic stress symptoms, depression, anxiety, stress, and life satisfaction.
Results: The present study reports secondary analyses. Correlational analyses within both trauma groups revealed positive relationships between avoidant coping and trauma-related symptoms. Group comparisons revealed higher levels of trauma-related symptoms, lower life satisfaction, and greater use of avoidant coping strategies in the interpersonal trauma group compared to the non-interpersonal trauma group. Furthermore, avoidant coping explained a significant proportion of variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms within both trauma groups, and trauma type itself accounted for significant unique variance in posttraumatic stress symptoms across the entire sample. Finally, exploratory mediation analyses provided evidence consistent with a partial mediation of the association between trauma type and posttraumatic stress symptoms.
Conclusions: These findings suggest that avoidant coping may be an important factor in understanding differences in trauma-related symptoms across trauma types. The results also point to potential intervention and prevention targets that should be evaluated in longitudinal research.

title: Principled Default Priors for Bayesian Hypothesis Testing in Binomial GLMs:   A Matched-Prior Framework with Empirical Evaluation
description: Researchers using Bayesian hypothesis testing in binomial generalized linear models (GLMs; for instance, logistic regression for accuracy data) need priors on regression coefficients, but no widely adopted default framework exists. The nonlinear link function creates a prior-link interaction: priors that appear reasonable on the coefficient scale can imply implausible distributions on the probability scale. We show that this problem has an exact solution for CDF-based link functions: matched priors – prior families whose CDF mirrors the link function – produce uniform distributions on the probability scale for the intercept, a maximally uninformative anchor. We extend this family choice to effect priors for parsimony, with prior scale determined via prior predictive simulation. We embed this matched-prior principle in a three-step workflow and evaluate it via simulation: (1) identify matched priors, (2) evaluate prior predictive distributions, (3) calibrate Bayes factors and compare to the JZS/Cauchy default. For logistic regression, the matched Logistic(0, 0.75) intercept prior and matched Logistic effect priors with scale 0.25 to 0.30 provide well-calibrated Bayes factors. A signal detection analysis shows that prior scale is a criterion parameter: it shifts the operating point on the ROC curve but does not change discriminability. The advantage of the matched-prior workflow is principled scale selection on the probability scale, where effect sizes are directly interpretable. The workflow extends naturally to hierarchical models, where the prior on the random-effects variance has negligible influence on Bayes factors for fixed effects. Ready-to-use brms code and scaling formulas for any contrast coding are provided.

title: Effects of Cognitive Warmup on Cognitive Performance
description: Cognitive performance is composed of a relatively stable cognitive ability (trait) and transient factors (state). While the trait can be influenced by long-term training, transient factors can be manipulated to achieve short-term improvements. Acute physical exercise has shown to improve subsequent cognitive performance through a warm-up effect; however, it remains unclear whether a similar enhancement can be achieved through cognitive activity itself. The present study investigated whether a brief cognitive warm-up could influence subsequent performance on a neuropsychological measure of processing speed. Forty healthy young adults participated in two experimental sessions and were assigned either to a cognitive warm-up group or a no-warm-up control group. The warm-up consisted of a brief series of serious games targeting executive functions and attention. Cognitive performance was assessed using the Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT). Bayesian analyses indicated an interaction between session and group, with better results for the warm-up group in the active session with respect to the control group, net of scores in Raven’s Matrices. These findings provide preliminary evidence that a brief cognitive warm-up may enhance subsequent performance on a speeded cognitive task. Importantly, the effect emerged in the absence of task-specific priming and did not rely on physical exercise, suggesting that cognitive performance may be influenced through transient modulation of cognitive state. Further research employing active control conditions, larger samples, and direct measures of underlying mechanisms is needed to establish the robustness and specificity of this effect.

title: Working with Circular Data: A Tutorial for Cognitive and Behavioral Research
description: Introductory statistics courses and conventional methods in behavioral and cognitive science are typically restricted to Euclidean space without explicitly stating this, leaving many researchers unaware of how to handle data with circular or periodic properties (e.g., time of day or orientation on a screen). This tutorial provides an accessible yet rigorous introduction, designed to bridge the gap between statistical theory and practical application. After discussing different ways of representing circular data mathematically and fundamental principles for working with them, we explain angular and vector representations and operations, including key considerations for implementing them in computer code. We then survey methods for visualizing circular data, including linear and polar plots and presentation of summary statistics.
